Basic Economic Concepts.skill: MEA-1.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.1) Basic Economic Concepts - 1.1 Scarcity
Basic Economic Concepts.prompt: This topic covers scarcity for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MEA-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.1). Students should understand that scarcity is the fundamental economic problem: human wants are unlimited, but resources are limited. Because of scarcity, individuals, firms, and governments must make choices about how to allocate scarce resources among competing uses. Resources (factors of production) include land (natural resources), labor (human effort), capital (manufactured goods used in production), and entrepreneurship (risk-taking and innovation). Every choice involves a trade-off, meaning an alternative is given up. Economics is the study of how agents make decisions under conditions of scarcity. Connect to opportunity cost, the production possibilities curve, and how scarcity drives all economic decision-making at both the micro and macro levels. AP exam note: scarcity is the conceptual foundation for the entire course and is embedded in all subsequent units. Vocabulary: scarcity, factors of production, land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship, trade-off, allocation, economics.

Basic Economic Concepts.skill: MOD-1.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.2) Basic Economic Concepts - 1.2 Opportunity Cost and the Production Possibilities Curve
Basic Economic Concepts.prompt: This topic covers opportunity cost and the production possibilities curve for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MOD-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.2). Students should understand that opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative forgone when a choice is made. The Production Possibilities Curve (PPC) models the maximum combinations of two goods an economy can produce given its resources and technology. Points on the curve are productively efficient; points inside are inefficient; points outside are currently unattainable. A bowed-out PPC reflects increasing opportunity costs due to specialized resources. Shifts of the PPC represent economic growth (outward) or decline (inward) caused by changes in resources or technology. A straight-line PPC indicates constant opportunity costs. Students must calculate opportunity cost from a PPC table or graph. Connect to comparative advantage, trade, and long-run aggregate supply. AP exam note: PPC analysis is heavily tested in both multiple-choice and free-response sections. Vocabulary: opportunity cost, production possibilities curve, productive efficiency, allocative efficiency, economic growth, constant vs. increasing opportunity costs.

Basic Economic Concepts.skill: MOD-1.B (Unit 1, Topic 1.3) Basic Economic Concepts - 1.3 Comparative Advantage and Trade
Basic Economic Concepts.prompt: This topic covers comparative advantage and trade for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MOD-1.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.3). Students should understand that absolute advantage means producing more output with the same resources, while comparative advantage means producing a good at a lower opportunity cost. Trade is mutually beneficial when each party specializes in the good for which it has a comparative advantage. The terms of trade must fall between the opportunity costs of the two trading partners for both to gain. Specialization and trade allow countries to consume beyond their individual PPCs. Students must calculate opportunity costs from production data to determine comparative advantage and identify acceptable terms of trade. Connect to international trade policy, exchange rates, and the gains from globalization discussed in Unit 6. AP exam note: comparative advantage calculations and determining terms of trade are classic free-response questions. Vocabulary: absolute advantage, comparative advantage, specialization, terms of trade, gains from trade, output method, input method.

Basic Economic Concepts.skill: MKT-1.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.4) Basic Economic Concepts - 1.4 Demand
Basic Economic Concepts.prompt: This topic covers demand for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MKT-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.4). Students should understand the law of demand: as price rises, quantity demanded falls, ceteris paribus, creating a downward-sloping demand curve. This inverse relationship is driven by the substitution effect and income effect. A change in price causes a movement along the demand curve (change in quantity demanded), while a change in a non-price determinant causes a shift of the entire demand curve (change in demand). Determinants of demand include consumer income (normal vs. inferior goods), prices of related goods (substitutes and complements), consumer tastes, expectations of future prices, and number of buyers. Students must distinguish between shifts of and movements along the demand curve. Connect to market equilibrium, aggregate demand, and the foreign exchange market. AP exam note: correctly identifying shifts vs. movements is critical on the exam. Vocabulary: law of demand, quantity demanded, demand schedule, demand curve, substitution effect, income effect, normal good, inferior good, substitutes, complements, ceteris paribus.

Basic Economic Concepts.skill: MKT-1.B (Unit 1, Topic 1.5) Basic Economic Concepts - 1.5 Supply
Basic Economic Concepts.prompt: This topic covers supply for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MKT-1.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.5). Students should understand the law of supply: as price rises, quantity supplied rises, ceteris paribus, creating an upward-sloping supply curve. A change in price causes a movement along the supply curve (change in quantity supplied), while a change in a non-price determinant shifts the entire supply curve (change in supply). Determinants of supply include input prices (costs of production), technology, expectations of future prices, number of sellers, government policies (taxes and subsidies), and prices of related goods in production. An increase in supply shifts the curve rightward; a decrease shifts it leftward. Students must clearly distinguish between changes in supply and changes in quantity supplied. Connect to aggregate supply, production costs, and the role of government regulation. AP exam note: supply shifters and their graphical effects are commonly tested. Vocabulary: law of supply, quantity supplied, supply schedule, supply curve, input prices, technology, subsidy, tax, determinants of supply.

Basic Economic Concepts.skill: MKT-1.C (Unit 1, Topic 1.6) Basic Economic Concepts - 1.6 Market Equilibrium, Disequilibrium, and Changes in Equilibrium
Basic Economic Concepts.prompt: This topic covers market equilibrium, disequilibrium, and changes in equilibrium for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MKT-1.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.6). Students should understand that equilibrium occurs where the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied, determining the market-clearing price and quantity. A surplus (excess supply) exists when price is above equilibrium, causing downward pressure on price. A shortage (excess demand) exists when price is below equilibrium, causing upward pressure on price. Markets tend toward equilibrium through price adjustments. When demand or supply shifts, a new equilibrium price and quantity result. Students must analyze simultaneous shifts in both demand and supply, recognizing that one variable (price or quantity) becomes indeterminate without knowing the relative magnitudes of the shifts. Connect to the AD-AS model, where aggregate equilibrium is determined similarly. AP exam note: double-shift analysis is a key tested skill; always state which outcome is indeterminate. Vocabulary: equilibrium price, equilibrium quantity, surplus, shortage, disequilibrium, market-clearing, simultaneous shifts, indeterminate.

Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.skill: MEA-1.B (Unit 2, Topic 2.1) Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle - 2.1 The Circular Flow and GDP
Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.prompt: This topic covers the circular flow and GDP for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MEA-1.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.1). Students should understand that the circular flow diagram models the flow of goods, services, resources, and money between households, firms, the government, the financial sector, and the foreign sector. Gross Domestic Product (GDP) measures the total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a given year. GDP can be calculated using the expenditure approach: GDP = C + I + G + (X - M), where C is consumer spending, I is gross private domestic investment, G is government spending, and (X - M) is net exports. The income approach sums all factor payments (wages, rent, interest, profits). Only final goods are counted to avoid double counting; intermediate goods are excluded. Transfer payments and purely financial transactions are not included in GDP. Connect to national income accounting, unemployment, and the business cycle. AP exam note: calculating GDP using the expenditure approach is a core skill. Vocabulary: GDP, circular flow, expenditure approach, income approach, final goods, intermediate goods, transfer payments, net exports, gross investment.

Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.skill: MEA-1.C (Unit 2, Topic 2.2) Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle - 2.2 Limitations of GDP
Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.prompt: This topic covers limitations of GDP for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MEA-1.C, Unit 2, Topic 2.2). Students should understand that while GDP is the primary measure of economic output, it has significant limitations as a measure of well-being. GDP does not account for non-market activities (household production, volunteer work), the underground economy (unreported transactions), the distribution of income, environmental degradation, quality of life factors (leisure, health, education), or changes in product quality. GDP per capita provides a better per-person comparison across countries but still misses these factors. Alternative measures such as the Human Development Index (HDI) attempt to address some shortcomings. Students must evaluate scenarios to determine what is and is not counted in GDP. Connect to economic growth, real vs. nominal GDP, and the broader goals of economic policy beyond output maximization. AP exam note: questions frequently test whether specific transactions are included in GDP. Vocabulary: GDP limitations, non-market activities, underground economy, income distribution, GDP per capita, externalities, quality of life.

Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.skill: MEA-2.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.3) Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle - 2.3 Unemployment
Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.prompt: This topic covers unemployment for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MEA-2.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.3). Students should understand that the unemployment rate equals the number of unemployed divided by the labor force, expressed as a percentage. The labor force includes employed persons plus those actively seeking work; it excludes discouraged workers, students, retirees, and institutionalized individuals. There are three types of unemployment: frictional (job transitions), structural (skills mismatch or geographic immobility), and cyclical (caused by economic downturns). The natural rate of unemployment (NRU) equals frictional plus structural unemployment and exists even at full employment. Full employment means zero cyclical unemployment, not zero total unemployment. The labor force participation rate measures the percentage of the working-age population in the labor force. Students must calculate unemployment rates and identify types from scenarios. Connect to the Phillips curve, the business cycle, and LRAS. AP exam note: distinguishing types of unemployment and calculating the rate are frequently tested. Vocabulary: unemployment rate, labor force, frictional unemployment, structural unemployment, cyclical unemployment, natural rate of unemployment, full employment, discouraged workers, labor force participation rate.

Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.skill: MEA-3.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.4) Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle - 2.4 Price Indices and Inflation
Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.prompt: This topic covers price indices and inflation for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MEA-3.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.4). Students should understand that inflation is a sustained increase in the general price level, measured using price indices. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) tracks the cost of a fixed market basket of goods and services purchased by a typical urban consumer over time. The inflation rate is calculated as the percentage change in the CPI: [(CPI_new - CPI_old) / CPI_old] x 100. The GDP deflator is a broader measure covering all goods and services in GDP, not just consumer goods. Students must calculate CPI from market basket data and compute inflation rates between periods. CPI can overstate inflation due to substitution bias, new product bias, and quality change bias. Deflation is a sustained decrease in the general price level and can be economically damaging. Connect to real vs. nominal values, the costs of inflation, and aggregate demand-aggregate supply analysis. AP exam note: CPI calculations and inflation rate computations are standard exam questions. Vocabulary: inflation, Consumer Price Index, GDP deflator, market basket, base year, inflation rate, deflation, disinflation, substitution bias.

Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.skill: MEA-3.B (Unit 2, Topic 2.5) Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle - 2.5 Costs of Inflation
Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.prompt: This topic covers costs of inflation for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MEA-3.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.5). Students should understand that inflation imposes real costs on the economy. Unanticipated inflation redistributes wealth from lenders to borrowers (since loans are repaid with less valuable dollars) and from those on fixed incomes to those with flexible incomes. Menu costs are the expenses of changing listed prices. Shoe-leather costs arise from the effort to minimize cash holdings during high inflation. Inflation creates uncertainty that discourages long-term investment and contracts. Anticipated inflation, if built into contracts and interest rates, causes fewer redistributive effects but still generates menu and shoe-leather costs. Hyperinflation destroys the functions of money and can collapse an economy. The real interest rate (Fisher equation: real = nominal - inflation) is what matters for economic decisions. Connect to monetary policy, the Phillips curve, and the distinction between demand-pull and cost-push inflation. AP exam note: identifying winners and losers from unanticipated inflation is a common question. Vocabulary: unanticipated inflation, anticipated inflation, menu costs, shoe-leather costs, wealth redistribution, fixed income, hyperinflation, Fisher equation.

Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.skill: MEA-3.C (Unit 2, Topic 2.6) Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle - 2.6 Real vs. Nominal GDP
Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.prompt: This topic covers real vs. nominal GDP for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MEA-3.C, Unit 2, Topic 2.6). Students should understand that nominal GDP measures output using current-year prices and therefore can increase due to either higher output or higher prices. Real GDP adjusts for inflation by using constant base-year prices, providing a more accurate measure of actual economic growth. Real GDP = (Nominal GDP / GDP Deflator) x 100. The GDP deflator is a price index that covers all goods and services produced domestically. When the GDP deflator is above 100, the price level has risen since the base year; when below 100, it has fallen. In the base year, nominal GDP equals real GDP and the deflator equals 100. Students must convert between nominal and real GDP and interpret changes in each. Real GDP per capita is the best measure for comparing living standards over time and across countries. Connect to economic growth, the business cycle, and aggregate demand-aggregate supply analysis. AP exam note: converting between real and nominal GDP using the deflator is a frequently tested calculation. Vocabulary: nominal GDP, real GDP, GDP deflator, base year, price level, real GDP per capita, inflation adjustment.

Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.skill: MEA-4.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.7) Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle - 2.7 Business Cycles
Economic Indicators and the Business Cyc.prompt: This topic covers business cycles for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MEA-4.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.7). Students should understand that business cycles are recurring fluctuations in real GDP around the long-run growth trend. The four phases are expansion (rising real GDP, falling unemployment), peak (maximum output before a downturn), contraction/recession (declining real GDP for two or more consecutive quarters, rising unemployment), and trough (minimum output before recovery). Leading economic indicators (stock prices, building permits, consumer expectations) change before the economy shifts phases. Lagging indicators (unemployment rate, CPI) change after. Coincident indicators (industrial production, personal income) move with the economy. During expansions, actual GDP may exceed potential GDP, creating an inflationary gap; during recessions, actual GDP falls below potential, creating a recessionary gap. Connect to aggregate demand-aggregate supply, fiscal and monetary policy responses, and the Phillips curve. AP exam note: identifying the phase of the business cycle and appropriate policy responses is a key skill. Vocabulary: business cycle, expansion, peak, contraction, recession, trough, leading indicators, lagging indicators, coincident indicators, inflationary gap, recessionary gap, potential GDP.

National Income and Price Determination.skill: MOD-2.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.1) National Income and Price Determination - 3.1 Aggregate Demand (AD)
National Income and Price Determination.prompt: This topic covers aggregate demand for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MOD-2.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.1). Students should understand that aggregate demand (AD) represents the total quantity of goods and services demanded at each price level, expressed as AD = C + I + G + (X - M). The AD curve slopes downward due to three effects: the wealth effect (higher prices reduce real wealth, decreasing consumption), the interest rate effect (higher prices increase money demand, raising interest rates and reducing investment), and the exchange rate effect (higher domestic prices make exports more expensive and imports cheaper, reducing net exports). Shifts in AD are caused by changes in any component: consumer confidence, investment spending, government purchases, tax policy, or net exports. An increase in AD shifts the curve rightward; a decrease shifts it leftward. Students must distinguish between movements along AD (caused by price level changes) and shifts of AD. Connect to fiscal policy, monetary policy, and the AD-AS model equilibrium. AP exam note: correctly shifting AD and explaining the reason is essential for free-response questions. Vocabulary: aggregate demand, wealth effect, interest rate effect, exchange rate effect, consumption, investment, government spending, net exports.

National Income and Price Determination.skill: MOD-2.B (Unit 3, Topic 3.2) National Income and Price Determination - 3.2 Multipliers
National Income and Price Determination.prompt: This topic covers multipliers for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MOD-2.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.2). Students should understand that the spending multiplier amplifies initial changes in spending into larger changes in real GDP. The spending multiplier = 1 / (1 - MPC) = 1 / MPS, where MPC is the marginal propensity to consume and MPS is the marginal propensity to save. MPC + MPS = 1. For example, if MPC = 0.8, the multiplier is 5, so a $10 billion increase in government spending would increase GDP by $50 billion (before price level adjustments). The tax multiplier is smaller in absolute value: tax multiplier = -MPC / (1 - MPC) = -MPC / MPS. Tax changes have a smaller effect than equal spending changes because part of a tax cut is saved. The balanced budget multiplier equals 1: equal increases in government spending and taxes increase GDP by the amount of the spending change. Students must calculate and apply all three multipliers. Connect to fiscal policy effectiveness and the AD-AS model. AP exam note: multiplier calculations are among the most frequently tested quantitative skills. Vocabulary: spending multiplier, tax multiplier, balanced budget multiplier, marginal propensity to consume, marginal propensity to save, multiplier effect.

National Income and Price Determination.skill: MOD-2.C (Unit 3, Topic 3.3) National Income and Price Determination - 3.3 Short-Run Aggregate Supply (SRAS)
National Income and Price Determination.prompt: This topic covers short-run aggregate supply for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MOD-2.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.3). Students should understand that the short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) curve shows the total quantity of goods and services firms are willing to produce at each price level in the short run. SRAS slopes upward because in the short run, some input prices (especially wages) are sticky and do not adjust immediately to changes in the price level. When the price level rises while input costs remain fixed, firms earn higher profits and increase output. Shifts of SRAS are caused by changes in input prices (wages, energy, raw materials), productivity, supply shocks (natural disasters, geopolitical events), business taxes, subsidies, and government regulations. An increase in SRAS shifts the curve rightward (lower price level, higher output); a decrease shifts it leftward (stagflation). Students must identify SRAS shifters and their effects on price level and real GDP. Connect to cost-push inflation, the AD-AS model, and long-run self-adjustment. AP exam note: distinguishing SRAS shifts from AD shifts is critical for correct analysis. Vocabulary: short-run aggregate supply, sticky wages, input prices, supply shock, stagflation, productivity, per-unit production costs.

National Income and Price Determination.skill: MOD-2.D (Unit 3, Topic 3.4) National Income and Price Determination - 3.4 Long-Run Aggregate Supply (LRAS)
National Income and Price Determination.prompt: This topic covers long-run aggregate supply for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MOD-2.D, Unit 3, Topic 3.4). Students should understand that the long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) curve is vertical at the full-employment level of output (potential GDP or Yf). In the long run, all prices and wages are fully flexible, so changes in the price level do not affect real output. The economy's long-run output depends solely on the quantity and quality of resources (land, labor, capital) and technology, not on the price level. LRAS shifts rightward with economic growth: increases in the labor force, capital stock, natural resources, or technological advancement. LRAS shifts leftward if resources are permanently depleted or destroyed. The position of LRAS corresponds to the natural rate of unemployment and full employment. Students must graph LRAS as a vertical line at potential GDP. Connect to the production possibilities curve (PPC shifts correspond to LRAS shifts), the business cycle, and long-run economic growth. AP exam note: always label LRAS at Yf and show it as perfectly vertical. Vocabulary: long-run aggregate supply, potential GDP, full employment output, natural rate of unemployment, economic growth, vertical LRAS.

National Income and Price Determination.skill: MOD-2.E (Unit 3, Topic 3.5) National Income and Price Determination - 3.5 Equilibrium in the Aggregate Demand-Aggregate Supply (AD-AS) Model
National Income and Price Determination.prompt: This topic covers equilibrium in the AD-AS model for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MOD-2.E, Unit 3, Topic 3.5). Students should understand that macroeconomic equilibrium occurs at the intersection of AD and SRAS, determining the equilibrium price level and real GDP. If equilibrium output is below potential GDP (left of LRAS), the economy has a recessionary gap with cyclical unemployment. If equilibrium output is above potential GDP (right of LRAS), the economy has an inflationary gap with upward pressure on wages and prices. When equilibrium output equals potential GDP, the economy is at full employment with no output gap. Students must draw a complete AD-AS diagram with correctly labeled axes (price level on vertical, real GDP on horizontal), AD, SRAS, and LRAS curves, and identify the equilibrium point, price level, and output. Connect to fiscal and monetary policy, the Phillips curve, and long-run self-adjustment. AP exam note: drawing and analyzing the AD-AS model is the most important graphical skill in the course. Vocabulary: macroeconomic equilibrium, recessionary gap, inflationary gap, full employment equilibrium, output gap, actual vs. potential GDP.

National Income and Price Determination.skill: MOD-2.F (Unit 3, Topic 3.6) National Income and Price Determination - 3.6 Changes in the AD-AS Model in the Short Run
National Income and Price Determination.prompt: This topic covers changes in the AD-AS model in the short run for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MOD-2.F, Unit 3, Topic 3.6). Students should understand how shifts in AD or SRAS create short-run changes in the price level, real GDP, and unemployment. An increase in AD (rightward shift) raises both the price level and real GDP, causing demand-pull inflation and reducing unemployment. A decrease in AD (leftward shift) lowers both price level and real GDP, increasing unemployment. A decrease in SRAS (leftward shift) raises the price level while reducing real GDP, creating stagflation (simultaneous inflation and recession). An increase in SRAS (rightward shift) lowers the price level while raising real GDP. Students must analyze how specific events (oil price shocks, tax changes, consumer confidence shifts) affect AD or SRAS and determine the resulting changes in output, price level, and unemployment. Connect to demand-pull vs. cost-push inflation, fiscal and monetary policy responses, and the Phillips curve. AP exam note: correctly identifying which curve shifts and the direction is the foundation of most macro free-response questions. Vocabulary: demand-pull inflation, cost-push inflation, stagflation, short-run equilibrium, negative supply shock, positive demand shock.

National Income and Price Determination.skill: MOD-2.G (Unit 3, Topic 3.7) National Income and Price Determination - 3.7 Long-Run Self-Adjustment
National Income and Price Determination.prompt: This topic covers long-run self-adjustment for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MOD-2.G, Unit 3, Topic 3.7). Students should understand that in the long run, the economy self-corrects back to full employment (potential GDP) through changes in wages and resource prices. In a recessionary gap, high unemployment puts downward pressure on wages, reducing production costs and shifting SRAS rightward until output returns to Yf at a lower price level. In an inflationary gap, low unemployment puts upward pressure on wages, increasing production costs and shifting SRAS leftward until output returns to Yf at a higher price level. The self-adjustment mechanism relies on the flexibility of wages and prices in the long run, even though they may be sticky in the short run. Classical economists emphasize this self-correcting tendency, while Keynesian economists argue the process is slow and costly, justifying government intervention. Students must illustrate self-adjustment graphically by showing SRAS shifts. Connect to the debate over active fiscal and monetary policy versus laissez-faire approaches. AP exam note: showing long-run self-adjustment on the AD-AS diagram is a key free-response skill. Vocabulary: long-run self-adjustment, wage flexibility, recessionary gap, inflationary gap, classical economics, Keynesian economics, self-correcting mechanism.

National Income and Price Determination.skill: POL-1.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.8) National Income and Price Determination - 3.8 Fiscal Policy
National Income and Price Determination.prompt: This topic covers fiscal policy for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: POL-1.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.8). Students should understand that fiscal policy involves changes in government spending and taxation to influence aggregate demand, implemented by Congress and the President. Expansionary fiscal policy (increased government spending, decreased taxes, or both) shifts AD rightward to combat recessions and reduce unemployment. Contractionary fiscal policy (decreased government spending, increased taxes, or both) shifts AD leftward to combat inflation. Government spending changes have a direct and larger multiplier effect on GDP than tax changes, because tax changes affect disposable income, part of which is saved. The spending multiplier is 1/MPS, and the tax multiplier is -MPC/MPS. Budget deficits occur when government spending exceeds tax revenue (typical during expansionary policy); budget surpluses occur when tax revenue exceeds spending (typical during contractionary policy). Students must recommend appropriate fiscal policy actions for given economic conditions. Connect to multipliers, crowding out, the national debt, and automatic stabilizers. AP exam note: recommending and graphing fiscal policy actions is a standard free-response task. Vocabulary: fiscal policy, expansionary, contractionary, budget deficit, budget surplus, government spending, taxation, discretionary policy.

National Income and Price Determination.skill: POL-1.B (Unit 3, Topic 3.9) National Income and Price Determination - 3.9 Automatic Stabilizers
National Income and Price Determination.prompt: This topic covers automatic stabilizers for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: POL-1.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.9). Students should understand that automatic stabilizers are government programs and tax structures that automatically adjust to stabilize the economy without requiring new legislation. Progressive income taxes automatically collect more revenue during expansions (restraining AD growth) and less during recessions (cushioning the decline in disposable income). Transfer payments such as unemployment insurance, welfare benefits, and food assistance automatically increase during recessions (supporting consumer spending) and decrease during expansions. These stabilizers reduce the severity of business cycle fluctuations by automatically increasing budget deficits during recessions and decreasing them (or creating surpluses) during expansions. Unlike discretionary fiscal policy, automatic stabilizers do not suffer from recognition, legislative, or implementation lags. However, they are generally insufficient to fully close output gaps on their own. Students must distinguish automatic stabilizers from discretionary fiscal policy. Connect to the business cycle, fiscal policy, and the debate over active stabilization. AP exam note: identifying automatic stabilizers and explaining their countercyclical mechanism is commonly tested. Vocabulary: automatic stabilizers, progressive tax, transfer payments, unemployment insurance, countercyclical, discretionary fiscal policy, recognition lag, implementation lag.

Financial Sector.skill: MKT-2.A (Unit 4, Topic 4.1) Financial Sector - 4.1 Financial Assets
Financial Sector.prompt: This topic covers financial assets for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MKT-2.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.1). Students should understand that financial assets are claims that entitle the owner to future income or value. The major categories include stocks (ownership shares in a company, offering dividends and capital gains but with higher risk), bonds (debt instruments where the borrower promises to pay interest and repay principal, with an inverse relationship between bond prices and interest rates), and loans (direct lending arrangements). When bond prices rise, interest rates fall, and vice versa; this inverse relationship is critical for understanding monetary policy transmission. The present value concept determines what a future payment is worth today, discounted by the interest rate. Liquidity refers to how easily an asset can be converted to cash without loss of value. The risk-return trade-off means higher expected returns require accepting greater risk. Students must understand the bond price-interest rate inverse relationship. Connect to the money market, loanable funds market, and monetary policy. AP exam note: the inverse relationship between bond prices and interest rates is heavily tested. Vocabulary: financial assets, stocks, bonds, interest rate, bond price, present value, liquidity, risk, return, dividends, principal, coupon rate.

Financial Sector.skill: MEA-3.D (Unit 4, Topic 4.2) Financial Sector - 4.2 Nominal vs. Real Interest Rates
Financial Sector.prompt: This topic covers nominal vs. real interest rates for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MEA-3.D, Unit 4, Topic 4.2). Students should understand that the nominal interest rate is the stated rate of return on a loan or financial asset without adjusting for inflation. The real interest rate adjusts for inflation and reflects the true cost of borrowing and true return on lending. The Fisher equation states: real interest rate = nominal interest rate - inflation rate (or expected inflation rate). Lenders and borrowers make decisions based on expected real interest rates. If inflation is higher than expected, borrowers gain and lenders lose purchasing power; if inflation is lower than expected, lenders gain and borrowers lose. The ex ante (expected) real rate uses expected inflation, while the ex post (actual) real rate uses realized inflation. Students must calculate real interest rates and determine how unexpected inflation affects lenders and borrowers. Connect to the money market, the loanable funds market, investment decisions, and international capital flows. AP exam note: applying the Fisher equation and analyzing the impact of unexpected inflation are standard test items. Vocabulary: nominal interest rate, real interest rate, Fisher equation, expected inflation, ex ante, ex post, purchasing power.

Financial Sector.skill: MEA-1.D (Unit 4, Topic 4.3) Financial Sector - 4.3 Definition, Measurement, and Functions of Money
Financial Sector.prompt: This topic covers the definition, measurement, and functions of money for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MEA-1.D, Unit 4, Topic 4.3). Students should understand that money serves three functions: medium of exchange (accepted for transactions, eliminating the need for barter), unit of account (standard measure of value for pricing goods and services), and store of value (retains purchasing power over time, though inflation erodes this function). Money must be durable, portable, divisible, uniformly acceptable, limited in supply, and difficult to counterfeit. The money supply is measured in categories: M1 includes the most liquid forms (currency in circulation, demand deposits/checkable deposits, traveler's checks), while M2 includes M1 plus near-money assets (savings deposits, money market accounts, small time deposits, money market mutual funds). Commodity money has intrinsic value; fiat money has value by government decree and is not backed by a physical commodity. Students must classify assets by money supply category. Connect to banking, the money market, monetary policy, and inflation. AP exam note: identifying the functions of money and components of M1 and M2 are commonly tested. Vocabulary: medium of exchange, unit of account, store of value, M1, M2, fiat money, commodity money, liquidity, demand deposits.

Financial Sector.skill: MKT-2.B (Unit 4, Topic 4.4) Financial Sector - 4.4 Banking and the Expansion of the Money Supply
Financial Sector.prompt: This topic covers banking and the expansion of the money supply for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MKT-2.B, Unit 4, Topic 4.4). Students should understand that commercial banks create money through the fractional reserve banking system. Banks are required to keep a fraction of deposits as required reserves and can lend out the remainder as excess reserves. When a bank makes a loan, new money is created as a demand deposit. This loaned money gets deposited in other banks, which can then lend a portion, creating a money multiplier process. The simple money (deposit) multiplier = 1 / required reserve ratio (RRR). For example, with a 10% reserve ratio, the multiplier is 10, meaning $1,000 in new deposits can potentially create up to $10,000 in total deposits. The actual expansion is typically less due to cash drains and banks holding excess reserves. Students must calculate required reserves, excess reserves, and the maximum change in the money supply from an initial deposit or Fed action. Connect to the money market, monetary policy tools, and interest rates. AP exam note: money multiplier calculations are among the most tested quantitative skills. Vocabulary: fractional reserve banking, required reserves, excess reserves, required reserve ratio, money multiplier, demand deposits, bank balance sheet, deposit expansion.

Financial Sector.skill: MKT-3.A (Unit 4, Topic 4.5) Financial Sector - 4.5 The Money Market
Financial Sector.prompt: This topic covers the money market for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MKT-3.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.5). Students should understand that the money market model shows the interaction between the supply of and demand for money, determining the nominal interest rate. The money supply (Sm) is a vertical curve set by the Federal Reserve, independent of the interest rate. The money demand (Dm) curve slopes downward because at higher interest rates, the opportunity cost of holding money increases, so people hold less money (preferring interest-bearing assets). Money demand shifts with changes in the price level, real GDP, or financial technology. An increase in money supply (rightward shift of Sm) lowers the nominal interest rate; a decrease raises it. When there is a surplus of money (interest rate above equilibrium), people buy bonds, driving bond prices up and interest rates down. When there is a shortage (rate below equilibrium), people sell bonds, driving bond prices down and interest rates up. Students must draw and analyze the money market diagram. Connect to monetary policy, the loanable funds market, and the AD-AS model. AP exam note: showing how Fed actions shift money supply and affect interest rates is a core free-response skill. Vocabulary: money market, money supply, money demand, nominal interest rate, opportunity cost, liquidity preference, bond prices.

Financial Sector.skill: POL-2.A (Unit 4, Topic 4.6) Financial Sector - 4.6 Monetary Policy
Financial Sector.prompt: This topic covers monetary policy for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: POL-2.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.6). Students should understand that monetary policy is conducted by the Federal Reserve (the Fed) to influence the money supply, interest rates, and ultimately aggregate demand. The Fed's primary tools are: open market operations (buying bonds increases money supply; selling bonds decreases it), the discount rate (the rate the Fed charges banks for short-term loans), and the reserve requirement (the percentage of deposits banks must hold). The federal funds rate is the rate banks charge each other for overnight loans and is the Fed's main policy target. Expansionary monetary policy (easy money) involves buying bonds, lowering the discount rate, or lowering the reserve requirement to increase the money supply, decrease interest rates, increase investment and consumption, and shift AD rightward. Contractionary monetary policy (tight money) does the opposite to fight inflation. Students must trace the complete transmission mechanism from Fed action to money supply to interest rates to investment to AD. Connect to the money market, AD-AS model, and the Phillips curve. AP exam note: the chain of causation from monetary policy to real GDP and price level is a classic free-response question. Vocabulary: Federal Reserve, open market operations, discount rate, reserve requirement, federal funds rate, expansionary monetary policy, contractionary monetary policy, transmission mechanism.

Financial Sector.skill: MKT-3.B (Unit 4, Topic 4.7) Financial Sector - 4.7 The Loanable Funds Market
Financial Sector.prompt: This topic covers the loanable funds market for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MKT-3.B, Unit 4, Topic 4.7). Students should understand that the loanable funds market models the interaction between borrowers (demand for loanable funds) and savers (supply of loanable funds), determining the real interest rate. The demand for loanable funds slopes downward: at lower real interest rates, more investment projects become profitable, increasing borrowing. The supply of loanable funds slopes upward: higher real interest rates incentivize more saving. Government borrowing (budget deficits) increases the demand for loanable funds, shifting demand rightward and raising real interest rates. Changes in saving behavior, foreign capital inflows, or government surpluses shift the supply curve. The loanable funds market differs from the money market: it determines the real interest rate (not nominal), represents the market for borrowing and lending (not liquidity), and is influenced by fiscal policy (not monetary policy). Students must draw and analyze the loanable funds market diagram. Connect to crowding out, fiscal policy, international capital flows, and the money market. AP exam note: distinguishing between the money market and loanable funds market is essential. Vocabulary: loanable funds market, real interest rate, supply of loanable funds, demand for loanable funds, saving, investment, government borrowing, budget deficit.

Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.skill: POL-3.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.1) Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies - 5.1 Fiscal and Monetary Policy Actions in the Short Run
Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.prompt: This topic covers fiscal and monetary policy actions in the short run for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: POL-3.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.1). Students should understand how fiscal and monetary policy interact and can be used together or independently to address economic fluctuations. In a recession, expansionary fiscal policy (increased G or decreased T) and expansionary monetary policy (increased money supply) both shift AD rightward, increasing real GDP and the price level while reducing unemployment. In an inflationary period, contractionary versions of both policies shift AD leftward. Fiscal policy directly changes AD through government spending or indirectly through taxes affecting disposable income. Monetary policy works through the interest rate transmission mechanism: changing the money supply affects interest rates, which affect investment and consumption, which shift AD. Policy mix decisions involve trade-offs: expansionary fiscal policy with contractionary monetary policy may maintain output while changing the composition of GDP (more government, less private investment). Students must recommend appropriate policy combinations for given economic scenarios. Connect to the AD-AS model, the money market, the loanable funds market, and the Phillips curve. AP exam note: multi-step policy analysis linking monetary and fiscal tools to outcomes is a signature free-response format. Vocabulary: policy mix, expansionary policy, contractionary policy, fiscal-monetary interaction, transmission mechanism, interest rate channel.

Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.skill: MOD-3.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.2) Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies - 5.2 The Phillips Curve
Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.prompt: This topic covers the Phillips curve for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MOD-3.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.2). Students should understand that the short-run Phillips curve (SRPC) shows an inverse relationship between the inflation rate and the unemployment rate. Moving along the SRPC corresponds to shifts in AD along a given SRAS curve: when AD increases, unemployment falls and inflation rises, and vice versa. The SRPC shifts when expected inflation or supply shocks change. An increase in expected inflation or an adverse supply shock shifts the SRPC upward (rightward); favorable supply conditions shift it downward. The long-run Phillips curve (LRPC) is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment (NRU), indicating no long-run trade-off between inflation and unemployment. In the long run, actual inflation adjusts to expected inflation, and unemployment returns to the NRU regardless of the inflation rate. The LRPC shifts when the NRU changes (due to structural factors). Students must draw both curves and show how policy actions move the economy along or shift the curves. Connect to AD-AS analysis, expectations theory, and the costs of disinflation. AP exam note: correctly graphing the Phillips curve alongside the AD-AS model is a frequently tested skill. Vocabulary: short-run Phillips curve, long-run Phillips curve, natural rate of unemployment, expected inflation, supply shock, inflation-unemployment trade-off, disinflation.

Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.skill: MEA-3.E (Unit 5, Topic 5.3) Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies - 5.3 Money Growth and Inflation
Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.prompt: This topic covers money growth and inflation for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MEA-3.E, Unit 5, Topic 5.3). Students should understand the quantity theory of money, expressed by the equation of exchange: MV = PQ (or MV = PY), where M is the money supply, V is the velocity of money (average times a dollar is spent on final goods per year), P is the price level, and Q (or Y) is real output. If velocity is stable and output is at full employment, increases in the money supply lead proportionally to increases in the price level (inflation). This provides the theoretical basis for the monetarist view that inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon. Excessive money growth beyond what is needed to support real economic growth causes sustained inflation. The quantity theory explains hyperinflation: governments that print money to finance deficits experience rapid price increases. Students must apply the equation of exchange to predict effects of changes in money supply on the price level. Connect to monetary policy, the long-run Phillips curve, and the costs of inflation. AP exam note: applying the quantity theory of money and its implications is a testable concept. Vocabulary: quantity theory of money, equation of exchange, velocity of money, money supply growth, price level, monetarism, hyperinflation.

Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.skill: POL-3.B (Unit 5, Topic 5.4) Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies - 5.4 Government Deficits and the National Debt
Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.prompt: This topic covers government deficits and the national debt for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: POL-3.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.4). Students should understand that a budget deficit occurs when government spending exceeds tax revenue in a single fiscal year, while the national debt is the accumulated total of all past deficits minus surpluses. The government finances deficits by borrowing, primarily through selling Treasury securities (bonds, notes, bills). Annual interest payments on the national debt are a significant and growing portion of the federal budget. The debt-to-GDP ratio is a better measure of debt sustainability than the absolute debt figure. Persistent deficits increase the national debt, raising concerns about crowding out of private investment, higher future taxes, reduced government flexibility, and potential inflationary pressures if debt is monetized (financed by money creation). Foreign-held debt involves transfer of interest payments abroad. Students must distinguish between the annual deficit and the accumulated national debt. Connect to fiscal policy, the loanable funds market, crowding out, and international capital flows. AP exam note: understanding the relationship between deficits, debt, and their economic consequences is essential. Vocabulary: budget deficit, budget surplus, national debt, Treasury securities, debt-to-GDP ratio, debt monetization, interest payments, fiscal sustainability.

Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.skill: POL-3.C (Unit 5, Topic 5.5) Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies - 5.5 Crowding Out
Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.prompt: This topic covers crowding out for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: POL-3.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.5). Students should understand that crowding out occurs when expansionary fiscal policy (increased government spending or tax cuts financed by borrowing) leads to higher interest rates, which reduce private investment and partially offset the intended increase in aggregate demand. The mechanism works as follows: government borrowing increases the demand for loanable funds, driving up real interest rates; higher interest rates discourage private investment spending (which is interest-rate sensitive); the reduction in investment partially offsets the increase in government spending, making fiscal policy less effective than the simple multiplier suggests. Complete crowding out (where the reduction in private investment equals the increase in government spending) is unlikely in practice, especially during recessions when there is ample saving. In the loanable funds market, crowding out is shown as a rightward shift of the demand curve, raising the real interest rate and reducing private investment. Students must illustrate crowding out graphically. Connect to the loanable funds market, the AD-AS model, fiscal policy effectiveness, and the national debt. AP exam note: demonstrating crowding out with the loanable funds market graph is a classic free-response question. Vocabulary: crowding out, government borrowing, real interest rate, private investment, loanable funds, fiscal policy effectiveness, deficit spending.

Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.skill: MEA-4.B (Unit 5, Topic 5.6) Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies - 5.6 Economic Growth
Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.prompt: This topic covers economic growth for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MEA-4.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.6). Students should understand that economic growth is a sustained increase in real GDP per capita over time, reflecting a rising standard of living. Growth is driven by increases in the quantity and quality of factors of production: growth in the labor force, accumulation of physical capital (investment in machinery, infrastructure), accumulation of human capital (education, training, health), technological progress and innovation, and improvements in institutional frameworks (property rights, rule of law). Economic growth is represented graphically by a rightward shift of both the LRAS curve and the PPC. The aggregate production function shows how inputs combine to produce output, with diminishing returns to individual factors but potential for sustained growth through technology. Productivity (output per worker or per hour) is the key driver of long-run growth in living standards. Real GDP per capita is the best measure for comparing growth across countries and over time. Students must identify sources of growth and their graphical representations. Connect to the PPC, LRAS, and policies that promote growth. AP exam note: identifying growth sources and showing them on PPC and AD-AS diagrams is commonly tested. Vocabulary: economic growth, real GDP per capita, productivity, human capital, physical capital, technological progress, aggregate production function, LRAS shift, PPC shift.

Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.skill: POL-3.D (Unit 5, Topic 5.7) Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies - 5.7 Public Policy and Economic Growth
Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization P.prompt: This topic covers public policy and economic growth for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: POL-3.D, Unit 5, Topic 5.7). Students should understand that governments can implement supply-side policies to promote long-run economic growth by shifting LRAS rightward. Key policy tools include: investment in education and workforce training (increasing human capital), investment in infrastructure (roads, bridges, broadband, increasing physical capital productivity), funding research and development (promoting technological innovation), tax incentives for business investment (accelerating capital accumulation), reducing regulatory burdens (lowering production costs), maintaining stable institutions and property rights (encouraging entrepreneurship and investment), and promoting free trade (expanding markets and specialization). Supply-side fiscal policy focuses on shifting LRAS, unlike demand-side fiscal policy that shifts AD. Tax cuts aimed at increasing labor supply and investment incentives are classic supply-side tools. These policies may take years to show results but have lasting effects on potential output. Students must evaluate how specific policies affect LRAS and potential GDP. Connect to economic growth, the PPC, aggregate supply, and the trade-offs between short-run stabilization and long-run growth. AP exam note: distinguishing demand-side from supply-side policy effects is an important analytical skill. Vocabulary: supply-side policy, human capital investment, infrastructure, research and development, tax incentives, deregulation, property rights, potential GDP, LRAS.

Open Economy - International Trade and F.skill: MOD-4.A (Unit 6, Topic 6.1) Open Economy - International Trade and Finance - 6.1 Balance of Payments Accounts
Open Economy - International Trade and F.prompt: This topic covers balance of payments accounts for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MOD-4.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Students should understand that the balance of payments (BOP) records all economic transactions between a country's residents and the rest of the world. It consists of two main accounts: the current account and the capital (financial) account. The current account tracks the flow of goods (trade balance), services, investment income, and net transfers (foreign aid, remittances). A current account deficit means the country imports more than it exports (in goods, services, and transfers combined). The capital (financial) account tracks the flow of financial assets: foreign direct investment, portfolio investment, and changes in official reserves. The fundamental BOP identity states that the current account balance plus the capital account balance must equal zero (they are mirror images). A current account deficit must be offset by a capital account surplus (net capital inflow), meaning the country borrows from or sells assets to foreigners. Students must classify transactions into the correct account and understand the BOP identity. Connect to exchange rates, net exports, and international capital flows. AP exam note: correctly categorizing BOP transactions and applying the BOP identity is commonly tested. Vocabulary: balance of payments, current account, capital account, financial account, trade balance, net transfers, capital inflow, capital outflow, BOP identity.

Open Economy - International Trade and F.skill: MKT-4.A (Unit 6, Topic 6.2) Open Economy - International Trade and Finance - 6.2 Exchange Rates
Open Economy - International Trade and F.prompt: This topic covers exchange rates for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MKT-4.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.2). Students should understand that an exchange rate is the price of one currency expressed in terms of another. Under a flexible (floating) exchange rate system, exchange rates are determined by supply and demand in the foreign exchange market. Currency appreciation means the currency increases in value relative to another (buys more foreign currency); depreciation means it decreases in value. Appreciation makes a country's exports more expensive and imports cheaper, reducing net exports. Depreciation makes exports cheaper and imports more expensive, increasing net exports. Under a fixed exchange rate system, the government or central bank maintains the currency at a set value by buying or selling currency reserves. Students must calculate exchange rates and determine the effects of appreciation and depreciation on trade. For example, if the exchange rate changes from 1 USD = 100 JPY to 1 USD = 120 JPY, the dollar has appreciated and the yen has depreciated. Connect to the foreign exchange market, net exports, aggregate demand, and international capital flows. AP exam note: correctly identifying appreciation vs. depreciation and their trade effects is essential. Vocabulary: exchange rate, appreciation, depreciation, flexible exchange rate, fixed exchange rate, floating, currency value, foreign exchange.

Open Economy - International Trade and F.skill: MKT-4.B (Unit 6, Topic 6.3) Open Economy - International Trade and Finance - 6.3 The Foreign Exchange Market
Open Economy - International Trade and F.prompt: This topic covers the foreign exchange market for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MKT-4.B, Unit 6, Topic 6.3). Students should understand that the foreign exchange (forex) market is where currencies are traded and exchange rates are determined under a flexible exchange rate system. The demand for a currency comes from foreigners wanting to buy the country's goods, services, or financial assets. The supply comes from domestic residents wanting to buy foreign goods, services, or assets. The equilibrium exchange rate is where quantity of currency demanded equals quantity supplied. The demand curve slopes downward: as the currency depreciates, the country's goods become less expensive to foreigners, increasing demand for the currency. The supply curve slopes upward: as the currency appreciates, foreign goods become cheaper for domestic buyers, increasing supply of domestic currency to the forex market. Students must draw the forex market diagram with the price of domestic currency (in terms of foreign currency) on the vertical axis and quantity of domestic currency on the horizontal axis. Connect to exchange rate determination, net exports, and capital flows. AP exam note: correctly drawing and labeling the forex market graph is a critical skill. Vocabulary: foreign exchange market, forex, currency demand, currency supply, equilibrium exchange rate, floating exchange rate, currency trading.

Open Economy - International Trade and F.skill: MKT-4.C (Unit 6, Topic 6.4) Open Economy - International Trade and Finance - 6.4 Effect of Changes in Policies and Economic Conditions on the Foreign Exchange Market
Open Economy - International Trade and F.prompt: This topic covers the effect of changes in policies and economic conditions on the foreign exchange market for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MKT-4.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.4). Students should understand that changes in economic conditions and policies shift the demand and supply curves in the forex market, causing currencies to appreciate or depreciate. Factors that increase demand for a country's currency (causing appreciation) include: higher domestic interest rates (attracting foreign capital), increased foreign demand for domestic goods, and increased foreign investment. Factors that increase supply of a country's currency (causing depreciation) include: higher domestic income (increasing import demand), higher domestic inflation relative to trading partners, and lower domestic interest rates (capital outflows). Expansionary monetary policy lowers domestic interest rates, causing capital outflows, increased supply of the domestic currency, and depreciation. Contractionary monetary policy has the opposite effect. Changes in tastes for foreign vs. domestic goods also shift demand and supply. Students must analyze how specific policy changes or economic events affect the forex market graph. Connect to monetary policy, fiscal policy, net exports, aggregate demand, and the balance of payments. AP exam note: tracing policy effects through the forex market to net exports and AD is a multi-step free-response task. Vocabulary: currency appreciation, currency depreciation, capital flows, relative interest rates, relative inflation, relative income, forex shifts.

Open Economy - International Trade and F.skill: MOD-4.B (Unit 6, Topic 6.5) Open Economy - International Trade and Finance - 6.5 Changes in the Foreign Exchange Market and Net Exports
Open Economy - International Trade and F.prompt: This topic covers changes in the foreign exchange market and net exports for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MOD-4.B, Unit 6, Topic 6.5). Students should understand the linkage between exchange rate changes and net exports, and ultimately aggregate demand. When a country's currency depreciates, its exports become cheaper for foreign buyers (increasing exports) and imports become more expensive for domestic buyers (decreasing imports), leading to an increase in net exports (X - M) and a rightward shift of AD. When a currency appreciates, exports become more expensive and imports become cheaper, decreasing net exports and shifting AD leftward. This creates a chain of effects: monetary policy changes affect interest rates, which affect exchange rates through capital flows, which affect net exports, which affect aggregate demand. For example, expansionary monetary policy lowers interest rates, causing capital outflows and currency depreciation, which increases net exports and further shifts AD rightward (reinforcing the initial policy effect). Students must trace this complete chain of causation across multiple graphs (money market, forex market, AD-AS model). Connect to monetary and fiscal policy, the balance of payments, and the AD-AS model. AP exam note: multi-graph analysis linking the money market, forex, and AD-AS is a signature AP Macro free-response question. Vocabulary: net exports, currency depreciation, currency appreciation, aggregate demand, exchange rate effects, trade balance, policy transmission.

Open Economy - International Trade and F.skill: MOD-4.C (Unit 6, Topic 6.6) Open Economy - International Trade and Finance - 6.6 Real Interest Rates and International Capital Flows
Open Economy - International Trade and F.prompt: This topic covers real interest rates and international capital flows for AP Macroeconomics students (CED: MOD-4.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.6). Students should understand that differences in real interest rates across countries drive international capital flows. Capital flows from countries with lower real interest rates to those with higher rates as investors seek the best risk-adjusted returns. When a country's real interest rate rises (due to fiscal or monetary policy), it attracts foreign capital (inflow), increasing demand for the domestic currency and causing appreciation. When domestic rates fall relative to foreign rates, capital flows out, increasing supply of domestic currency and causing depreciation. These flows link the loanable funds market, money market, and forex market. Government budget deficits that raise real interest rates can attract foreign capital, leading to currency appreciation and reduced net exports, creating a twin deficit (budget deficit and trade deficit). Students must connect domestic policy changes to capital flows, exchange rates, and trade balances. Connect to the loanable funds market, the forex market, and the AD-AS model. AP exam note: tracing the full chain from domestic policy to international effects across multiple markets is the most commonly tested multi-part free-response format. Vocabulary: international capital flows, real interest rate differential, capital inflow, capital outflow, twin deficits, financial capital mobility, currency appreciation, trade deficit.
